MENTAL DISORDER IS NOT A FASHION ⬇️

(Idea credits: @eemaanasif) 🫡🧠

Often times I have seen these huge celebrities labelling certain disorders like PCOS, OCD, ADHD, etc onto themselves without ANY basis or proper diagnosis. The reason is understandable. More talking points in media and PR. They have made a trend out of mental illnesses. 

ADHD is a serious problem. Those who have it especially children it is very evident from observation. Manifestations in adults is different. There are different types. Attention Deficit type. Hyperactive type. or Mixed. 

Lastly, stop labelling disorders onto yourself. It can only be concluded with proper diagnosis and analysis of symptoms

It was a huge honor and a personal achievement for me to appear as a guest speaker at @lifeatlums Orientation event and a massive thank you to @riayat_lums for making it possible for me. 

We had a healthy interactive session on urban planning and how we, as civilians, can make cities more walkable and suited to pedestrians. Meanwhile, we discussed thoroughly on how we can improve our mental health by adapting a healthy lifestyle and environment. 

Nevertheless, this is a place I would love to come back to.

Schizophrenia is a complex mental health disorder where people experience a different reality than others. It can make someone see, hear, or feel things that aren’t actually there, or believe things that don’t make sense to others. These experiences can be confusing and overwhelming, as the line between real and imagined becomes blurred.

There are different types of schizophrenia that affect people in various ways:

Paranoid Schizophrenia – This is the most common type, where people have strong beliefs that others are plotting against them or want to harm them, along with hearing voices.

Disorganized Schizophrenia – Here, a person’s thoughts and speech become jumbled, making it hard for them to communicate clearly. Their behavior may also become unpredictable.

Catatonic Schizophrenia – In this rare form, a person might stop moving or speaking for long periods or, in contrast, may move in unusual ways.

Undifferentiated Schizophrenia – This type includes symptoms from multiple forms, making it hard to categorize under just one type.

Schizophrenia is more than just hearing voices or having strange thoughts—it’s a lifelong condition that requires understanding and support. With the right help, people living with schizophrenia can still lead meaningful lives.

Here are 5 reasons you feel depressed all the time:

HIGH SUGAR INTAKE:
Bad news for 'Meetha' lovers. Overconsumption of sugar triggers imbalances in certain brain chemicals, especially when consumed in high amounts in the morning. These imbalances can lead to depression and may even increase the long-term risk of developing a mental health disorder in some people.

POOR SLEEPING ROUTINE:
Scrolling through reels late at night won't do you any good. Poor sleep may create difficulties regulating emotions that, in turn, may leave you more vulnerable to depression in the future — months or even years from now. And depression itself is associated with sleep difficulties such as shortening the amount of restorative slow wave sleep a person gets each night. 

PHYSICAL INACTIVITY:
This is the real pandemic. By not moving your body, you're limiting the oxygen supply to your brain and also preventing the natural release of Endorphins which are feel good chemicals of your body. Exercising keeps the brain active and is also correlated to having better sleep. 

SOCIAL ISOLATION:
Humans are social animals and we are meant to socialise and interact with others. And no! I'm not talking about social media. We must take our time out to interact with friends and family, to share our lives with others. Social disconnection leads to not only depression but also heart problems and reduced life expectancy. 

SMOKING: 
Smoking will give you a temporary feeling of sedation but will ultimately make you depressed in the long term. Smoking is a behavior that increases the risk of depression. The higher the smoking frequency and smoking volume, the higher the risk of depression, whereas smoking cessation is associated with decreased risk of depression, and the longer the smoking cessation duration, the lower the risk of depression.
